questions.question: What is async/await in C# and how does it work?
questions.answer:
async/await is C#'s way of writing asynchronous code that reads like synchronous code. The compiler transforms async methods into a state machine — the method is split at each await point, and the runtime resumes execution when the awaited operation completes.

Key points:

  • async keyword marks a method as asynchronous. It allows await inside it.
  • await suspends the current method without blocking the thread. The thread is returned to the thread pool to handle other work.
  • Return types should be Task (void equivalent), Task<T> (result), or ValueTask<T> (performance variant).
// Without async: thread blocked during HTTP call
public Invoice GetInvoice(int id) {
    return _httpClient.GetFromJson<Invoice>($"/api/invoices/{id}").Result; // BLOCKS!
}

// With async: thread free during HTTP call
public async Task<Invoice?> GetInvoiceAsync(int id) {
    return await _httpClient.GetFromJsonAsync<Invoice>($"/api/invoices/{id}");
}

In ASP.NET Core, every incoming request gets a thread. If that thread blocks on a DB call or HTTP call, it's wasted — can't handle other requests. Async frees it during the wait, enabling the same number of threads to handle far more concurrent requests.
followUps: What does await actually do under the hood?
What is the difference between async void and async Task?
What is a SynchronizationContext?

followUpAnswers.What does await actually do under the ho:
When the C# compiler sees await someTask, it transforms the entire async method into a state machine — a compiler-generated class that implements IAsyncStateMachine. Each await becomes a state transition point.

What happens at each await:

  1. The awaitable's GetAwaiter() is called.
  2. If IsCompleted is already true (task done synchronously), execution continues immediately — no suspension at all.
  3. If not completed, a continuation delegate is registered via OnCompleted() — this is the "rest of the method after the await".
  4. The current method returns control to its caller (the thread is freed).
  5. When the awaited operation finishes, the continuation is scheduled — typically back on the thread pool (or back to the SynchronizationContext if one was captured).
  6. The state machine resumes from where it left off.
// What you write:
public async Task<string> GetDataAsync()
{
    var response = await _httpClient.GetStringAsync(url);
    return response.ToUpper();
}

// What the compiler generates (simplified):
public Task<string> GetDataAsync()
{
    var stateMachine = new GetDataAsyncStateMachine { _this = this };
    stateMachine.MoveNext();  // runs until first await
    return stateMachine._taskBuilder.Task;
}

// The MoveNext switch-case handles each state (each await point)
// State 0: call _httpClient.GetStringAsync, if not done register continuation
// State 1 (continuation): receive response, do .ToUpper(), set result

Key insight: await is not a thread blocking instruction — it's a callback registration mechanism dressed up in readable syntax. No thread is consumed during the wait.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between async voi:
The return type of an async method fundamentally changes how exceptions propagate and how the caller interacts with it:

async Taskasync void
AwaitableYes — caller can await itNo — caller cannot await
Exception propagationException stored in Task, rethrown when awaitedException thrown on SynchronizationContext — crashes app
TestableYes — await the method in testNo — test can't know when it's done
Valid use caseEverywhereOnly event handlers
// async Task: exception propagates safely
public async Task ProcessAsync()
{
    throw new InvalidOperationException("Something failed");
}

try
{
    await ProcessAsync();  // exception caught here
}
catch (InvalidOperationException ex)
{
    _logger.LogError(ex, "Processing failed");  // handled
}

// async void: exception is UNHANDLED — crashes the process
public async void ProcessAsync()
{
    throw new InvalidOperationException("Something failed");  // app crashes!
}

// Can't catch this:
ProcessAsync();  // fire and forget, exception silently destroys the app

The only acceptable async void: event handlers where the delegate signature requires void:

// Acceptable — but wrap in try/catch
private async void Button_Click(object sender, EventArgs e)
{
    try
    {
        await DoWorkAsync();
    }
    catch (Exception ex)
    {
        MessageBox.Show(ex.Message);
    }
}
followUpAnswers.What is a SynchronizationContext?:
A SynchronizationContext represents a "where continuations should run" context. It's an abstraction that lets the async infrastructure know how to marshal work back to a specific thread or environment after an await completes.

Different environments have different SynchronizationContexts:

  • WinForms/WPF — UI SynchronizationContext: continuations must run on the UI thread (to safely update controls)
  • Old ASP.NET (not Core) — AspNetSynchronizationContext: continuations run on a thread with the original HttpContext
  • ASP.NET Core — no SynchronizationContext: continuations run on any thread pool thread
  • Console app — no SynchronizationContext: continuations run on any thread pool thread
// In WinForms, after an await, you're back on the UI thread:
private async void LoadButton_Click(object sender, EventArgs e)
{
    var data = await FetchDataAsync();  // thread suspended here
    labelResult.Text = data;  // safely on UI thread — SyncContext captured and restored
}

// How the capture works:
// Before awaiting: SynchronizationContext.Current is captured
// After the async op completes: Post() is called on the captured context
// This marshals the continuation back to the right thread

Why it matters for deadlocks: If you call .Result on a Task from a context that has a SynchronizationContext (blocking the context thread), and the async method tries to post its continuation back to that same context thread — you have a circular wait (deadlock).

ConfigureAwait(false) tells the awaiter not to capture and restore the SynchronizationContext — the continuation runs on whatever thread pool thread is available, avoiding this deadlock.

questions.tip: Never use .Result or .Wait() on a Task in ASP.NET Core — it blocks the thread AND can cause deadlocks in contexts with a SynchronizationContext (like old ASP.NET or WinForms). Always await.

questions.question: What causes a deadlock with async/await and how do you fix it?
questions.answer:
The classic deadlock pattern: calling .Result or .Wait() on a Task inside a context that has a SynchronizationContext (WinForms, old ASP.NET, unit test runners).

How it deadlocks:

  1. UI thread (SynchronizationContext) calls GetDataAsync().Result — blocks UI thread.
  2. GetDataAsync() completes and tries to post the continuation back to the UI thread's SynchronizationContext.
  3. But the UI thread is blocked waiting for GetDataAsync() to complete.
  4. Circular wait = deadlock.
// DEADLOCK in WinForms/old ASP.NET
public string GetData() {
    return GetDataAsync().Result;  // blocks context thread
}

// Fix 1: go async all the way
public async Task<string> GetData() {
    return await GetDataAsync();  // never block
}

// Fix 2: ConfigureAwait(false) — avoids SynchronizationContext
public async Task<string> GetDataAsync() {
    var data = await _httpClient.GetStringAsync(url)
        .ConfigureAwait(false);  // continuation runs on thread pool, not SyncContext
    return data;
}

In ASP.NET Core there's no SynchronizationContext, so .Result doesn't deadlock — but it still blocks the thread, hurting throughput.
followUps: What is ConfigureAwait(false) and when do you need it?
What is SynchronizationContext?

followUpAnswers.What is ConfigureAwait(false) and when d:
ConfigureAwait(false) tells the awaiter: "when this task completes, do not try to marshal the continuation back to the captured SynchronizationContext — just run on any available thread pool thread."

Default behavior (ConfigureAwait(true) — the default):

// The captured SynchronizationContext is restored after await
var result = await SomeAsyncMethod();  // equivalent to .ConfigureAwait(true)
// Continuation runs on the original context (UI thread, request context, etc.)

With ConfigureAwait(false):

var result = await SomeAsyncMethod().ConfigureAwait(false);
// Continuation runs on any thread pool thread — no context switch needed

When you NEED it:

  • Library code — libraries don't know what context their callers are using. Always use ConfigureAwait(false) in library code to avoid capturing caller's context and causing deadlocks.
  • Avoiding deadlocks — in WinForms/old ASP.NET when you can't go fully async (blocking caller exists in the call chain).

When you DON'T need it:

  • ASP.NET Core application code — no SynchronizationContext, so ConfigureAwait(false) has no effect on deadlock prevention (though it saves a tiny context-check overhead).
  • UI event handlers — you usually WANT to return to the UI thread after await.
// Library code: always ConfigureAwait(false)
public class MyLibraryClient
{
    public async Task<string> GetDataAsync(string url)
    {
        var response = await _httpClient.GetStringAsync(url).ConfigureAwait(false);
        var processed = await ProcessAsync(response).ConfigureAwait(false);
        return processed;
    }
}

// ASP.NET Core controller: ConfigureAwait(false) optional but harmless
public async Task<IActionResult> Get(int id)
{
    var result = await _service.GetAsync(id);  // no SyncContext anyway
    return Ok(result);
}
followUpAnswers.What is SynchronizationContext?:
A SynchronizationContext represents a "where continuations should run" context. It's an abstraction that lets the async infrastructure know how to marshal work back to a specific thread or environment after an await completes.

Different environments have different SynchronizationContexts:

  • WinForms/WPF — UI SynchronizationContext: continuations must run on the UI thread (to safely update controls)
  • Old ASP.NET (not Core) — AspNetSynchronizationContext: continuations run on a thread with the original HttpContext
  • ASP.NET Core — no SynchronizationContext: continuations run on any thread pool thread
  • Console app — no SynchronizationContext: continuations run on any thread pool thread
// Check current context:
var ctx = SynchronizationContext.Current;
// null in ASP.NET Core / console
// WindowsFormsSynchronizationContext in WinForms

// In WinForms, after an await, you're automatically back on the UI thread:
private async void LoadButton_Click(object sender, EventArgs e)
{
    var data = await FetchDataAsync();  // leaves UI thread during wait
    labelResult.Text = data;            // back on UI thread — safe to update UI
}

The deadlock mechanism: If you block a SynchronizationContext thread with .Result, and the async method tries to post its continuation back to that same thread, you get a circular wait. The thread is blocked waiting for the task, and the task is waiting for the blocked thread to become free.

ConfigureAwait(false) breaks this cycle by telling the continuation not to capture/restore the SynchronizationContext at all — it just uses the thread pool directly.

questions.tip: Library code should always use ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid capturing the caller's SynchronizationContext. Application code (controllers, UI code-behind) doesn't need it because there's an expectation to return to the original context.

questions.question: What is the difference between Task.WhenAll and Task.WhenAny?
questions.answer:
Both combine multiple Tasks but with different completion semantics:

Task.WhenAll — waits for ALL tasks to complete. If any throws, it rethrows after all complete (wraps multiple exceptions in AggregateException).

// Run 3 HTTP calls in parallel, wait for all
var portfolioTask = _portfolioSvc.GetAsync(id);
var holdingsTask = _holdingsSvc.GetByPortfolioAsync(id);
var perfTask = _performanceSvc.GetAsync(id);

await Task.WhenAll(portfolioTask, holdingsTask, perfTask);

var portfolio = await portfolioTask;  // already completed, just unwraps result
var holdings = await holdingsTask;
var perf = await perfTask;

Task.WhenAny — returns when the FIRST task completes. Useful for timeouts or racing multiple sources.

// Timeout pattern
var dataTask = _service.GetExpensiveDataAsync();
var timeoutTask = Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(5));

var first = await Task.WhenAny(dataTask, timeoutTask);
if (first == timeoutTask)
    throw new TimeoutException("Service did not respond in time");

var data = await dataTask;

In FolioTrack's portfolio dashboard, I used Task.WhenAll to load market data, portfolio details, and performance metrics concurrently — reduced page load from 1.5s (sequential) to 600ms (parallel).
followUps: How do you handle exceptions from Task.WhenAll?
What is Parallel.ForEachAsync?

followUpAnswers.How do you handle exceptions from Task.W:
Task.WhenAll waits for all tasks to finish — even if some fail. If multiple tasks throw, it wraps all exceptions in an AggregateException, but when you await it, only the first exception is re-thrown (the others are swallowed).

To see ALL exceptions, await individual tasks after WhenAll:

var task1 = FetchPortfolioAsync(1);
var task2 = FetchPortfolioAsync(2);
var task3 = FetchPortfolioAsync(3);

try
{
    await Task.WhenAll(task1, task2, task3);
}
catch
{
    // WhenAll throws after ALL complete — even if some failed
    // But re-throwing from 'await' only surfaces the FIRST exception
    // Check each task individually:
    var exceptions = new List<Exception>();
    foreach (var task in new[] { task1, task2, task3 })
    {
        if (task.IsFaulted)
            exceptions.AddRange(task.Exception!.InnerExceptions);
    }
    // Handle all exceptions
    _logger.LogError("Multiple failures: {Count}", exceptions.Count);
    throw new AggregateException(exceptions);
}

Pattern for partial success (collect results + errors):

var tasks = portfolioIds.Select(id => FetchPortfolioAsync(id)).ToList();
await Task.WhenAll(tasks);  // wait for all, regardless of failures

var successful = tasks
    .Where(t => t.IsCompletedSuccessfully)
    .Select(t => t.Result)
    .ToList();

var failed = tasks
    .Where(t => t.IsFaulted)
    .Select(t => t.Exception!.InnerException!)
    .ToList();

_logger.LogInformation("{Ok} succeeded, {Fail} failed", successful.Count, failed.Count);
followUpAnswers.What is Parallel.ForEachAsync?:
Parallel.ForEachAsync (introduced in .NET 6) processes a collection in parallel with a configurable max degree of parallelism, and properly supports async lambdas. It's the async-native successor to Parallel.ForEach which was designed for CPU-bound synchronous work.

// Process invoices with at most 5 concurrent HTTP calls
var invoiceIds = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3, /* ... 500 more */ };

await Parallel.ForEachAsync(
    invoiceIds,
    new ParallelOptions { MaxDegreeOfParallelism = 5 },
    async (invoiceId, ct) =>
    {
        var status = await _apiClient.CheckInvoiceStatusAsync(invoiceId, ct);
        await _db.Invoices
            .Where(i => i.Id == invoiceId)
            .ExecuteUpdateAsync(s => s.SetProperty(i => i.Status, status), ct);
    }
);

Comparison with alternatives:

ApproachProsCons
Parallel.ForEachAsyncBuilt-in throttling, clean syntax, cancellation support.NET 6+ only
Task.WhenAll + SelectSimple, works everywhereNo built-in throttling (all tasks start at once)
SemaphoreSlim + Task.WhenAllFine-grained throttle controlMore boilerplate
// Equivalent using SemaphoreSlim (pre-.NET 6 or more control needed):
var semaphore = new SemaphoreSlim(5, 5);
var tasks = invoiceIds.Select(async id =>
{
    await semaphore.WaitAsync(ct);
    try { return await _apiClient.CheckInvoiceStatusAsync(id, ct); }
    finally { semaphore.Release(); }
});
await Task.WhenAll(tasks);

questions.tip: Task.WhenAll exception handling: Even if one task throws, WhenAll waits for all to complete before rethrowing. Catch AggregateException and check .InnerExceptions, or await each task individually after WhenAll to get per-task exceptions.

questions.question: What is CancellationToken and how do you use it?
questions.answer:
A CancellationToken is a cooperative cancellation mechanism — it signals that work should stop, but the work itself decides when and how to stop (check the token, clean up, throw OperationCanceledException).

How it flows in ASP.NET Core: The framework provides a CancellationToken per request (via HttpContext.RequestAborted). If the client disconnects, this token is cancelled. Thread it through to avoid wasted DB/HTTP work for disconnected clients.

// Controller — ASP.NET Core auto-binds CancellationToken
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Get(int id, CancellationToken ct)
{
    var result = await _service.GetAsync(id, ct);
    return Ok(result);
}

// Service — pass token to DB and HTTP calls
public async Task<Portfolio?> GetAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct)
{
    // EF Core respects CT
    return await _db.Portfolios
        .FirstOrDefaultAsync(p => p.Id == id, ct);
}

// Creating your own cancellation
using var cts = new CancellationTokenSource(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10));
try {
    var data = await _service.GetAsync(id, cts.Token);
} catch (OperationCanceledException) {
    // timed out
}

projectExample.explanation: BackgroundService uses CancellationToken to stop the loop cleanly when the host is shutting down — Task.Delay with the token cancels immediately instead of waiting the full hour.

followUps: What happens if you ignore a CancellationToken?
How do you link two CancellationTokens?

followUpAnswers.What happens if you ignore a Cancellatio:
If you ignore a CancellationToken, the operation continues running even after cancellation has been requested — wasting resources, holding DB connections, and delaying shutdown.

Concrete consequences:

  • Client disconnects — your EF Core query and HTTP calls continue for a user who's already gone. DB connections are held longer than necessary.
  • Application shutdown is delayed — BackgroundService stopping is delayed because your loop doesn't check the token. The host waits (up to a configurable timeout) before force-killing.
  • Timeout doesn't cancel the inner work — if you create a CancellationTokenSource with a timeout but don't pass the token to EF Core/HttpClient, the timeout has no effect on the actual I/O.
// BAD: token not passed — operation runs to completion even if cancelled
public async Task<List<Invoice>> GetInvoicesAsync(CancellationToken ct)
{
    // ct is never used — query runs even if request is aborted
    return await _db.Invoices.ToListAsync();  // missing ct!
}

// GOOD: token passed through
public async Task<List<Invoice>> GetInvoicesAsync(CancellationToken ct)
{
    return await _db.Invoices.ToListAsync(ct);  // cancels the DB call if ct fires
}

// BAD: BackgroundService ignoring stoppingToken
protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
{
    while (true)  // never stops!
    {
        await DoWorkAsync();  // stoppingToken not passed
        await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5));  // won't exit early on shutdown
    }
}

// GOOD:
protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
{
    while (!stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)
    {
        await DoWorkAsync(stoppingToken);
        await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5), stoppingToken);
    }
}
followUpAnswers.How do you link two CancellationTokens?:
CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource() creates a new token that is cancelled when any of the linked tokens is cancelled. This is the standard pattern for combining an external cancellation signal with a local timeout.

// Common pattern: combine request timeout with client disconnect detection
public async Task<Report> GenerateReportAsync(
    ReportRequest req, CancellationToken requestAborted)
{
    // Cancel if: client disconnects OR 30-second timeout exceeded
    using var cts = CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource(
        requestAborted,
        new CancellationTokenSource(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30)).Token);

    try
    {
        var data = await _db.Reports
            .Where(r => r.Year == req.Year)
            .ToListAsync(cts.Token);  // respects both cancellation sources

        var pdf = await _pdfGenerator.BuildAsync(data, cts.Token);
        return pdf;
    }
    catch (OperationCanceledException) when (requestAborted.IsCancellationRequested)
    {
        throw;  // client disconnected — propagate
    }
    catch (OperationCanceledException)
    {
        throw new TimeoutException("Report generation timed out");
    }
}

// Linking three tokens:
using var cts = CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource(
    userToken,
    timeoutToken,
    globalShutdownToken);

Important: Dispose the linked CancellationTokenSource when done (the using statement handles this). Failing to dispose linked token sources is a common memory/resource leak, especially in long-running loops.

questions.tip: Always pass CancellationToken through your entire call chain. Not passing it to EF Core or HttpClient means the DB query/HTTP call continues even after the client disconnects — wasted compute and DB connections.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Thread, Task, and async/await?
questions.answer:
Three different levels of abstraction for concurrency:

Thread — an OS-level unit of execution. Heavy: ~1MB stack, kernel objects, context switch overhead. Creating threads manually is rare in modern .NET. Use when you need true CPU parallelism or specific thread affinity.

Task — a higher-level abstraction representing a unit of work. Tasks run on thread pool threads (not dedicated threads). Lightweight to create. The Task Parallel Library (TPL) manages scheduling, continuations, and cancellation. Task.Run() runs CPU-bound work on the thread pool.

async/await — a language-level abstraction for writing asynchronous code. async/await doesn't create threads. For I/O-bound work (DB, HTTP, file), await suspends the method and returns the thread to the pool while waiting — no thread is consumed during the wait.

When to use what:

  • I/O-bound work (DB query, HTTP call): async/await — no thread needed while waiting.
  • CPU-bound work (image processing, complex computation): Task.Run(() => DoHeavyWork()) — offload to thread pool to not block the calling thread.
  • Thread: Rarely. Background agents with specific requirements (STA thread for COM interop, specific CPU affinity).
followUps: What is the thread pool?
When would you use Task.Run in ASP.NET Core?

followUpAnswers.What is the thread pool?:
The thread pool is a managed pool of reusable threads maintained by the .NET runtime. Instead of creating and destroying threads for every unit of work (expensive — ~1MB stack allocation, OS kernel objects), the thread pool keeps a set of threads alive and recycles them.

How it works:

  • When you Task.Run(), ThreadPool.QueueUserWorkItem(), or schedule a continuation, the work item goes into the thread pool queue.
  • An idle thread picks it up and executes it.
  • When done, the thread returns to the pool (not destroyed).
  • If all threads are busy and the queue grows, the pool gradually creates new threads (one per second by default) up to the configured max.
// Check thread pool state
ThreadPool.GetAvailableThreads(out int workerThreads, out int completionPortThreads);
ThreadPool.GetMaxThreads(out int maxWorker, out int maxIOCP);
Console.WriteLine($"Available: {workerThreads}/{maxWorker} workers");

// Configure minimum threads (important for burst workloads)
// Default min is one per CPU core — can cause "thread starvation" under heavy load
ThreadPool.SetMinThreads(workerThreads: 50, completionPortThreads: 50);

Thread starvation — a dangerous condition where all thread pool threads are blocked (waiting synchronously on I/O), new work can't execute, and the pool is slow to add new threads. Symptoms: requests queuing up, response times increasing, CPU low. Fix: use async/await properly — never block thread pool threads on I/O.
followUpAnswers.When would you use Task.Run in ASP.NET C:
Task.Run offloads synchronous, CPU-bound work to the thread pool, freeing the calling thread (the request thread) to do other things. In ASP.NET Core, the legitimate uses are narrow but important:

When to use Task.Run:

  • CPU-intensive synchronous work that you cannot make async — e.g., third-party library that does heavy computation synchronously, image resizing, PDF generation, cryptographic operations on large data.
  • Parallelizing multiple CPU-bound operations — run several expensive calculations concurrently.
// Legitimate: CPU-bound work (PDF library is synchronous)
[HttpPost("generate-pdf")]
public async Task<IActionResult> GeneratePdf(ReportRequest req)
{
    // Without Task.Run: request thread tied up doing CPU work
    // With Task.Run: request thread is freed; a pool thread does the CPU work
    var pdfBytes = await Task.Run(() => _pdfLibrary.Generate(req));
    return File(pdfBytes, "application/pdf");
}

// Legitimate: parallelizing multiple CPU-bound ops
var task1 = Task.Run(() => CalculateMetrics(portfolio1));
var task2 = Task.Run(() => CalculateMetrics(portfolio2));
await Task.WhenAll(task1, task2);

When NOT to use Task.Run in ASP.NET Core:

  • I/O-bound work — Task.Run(() => _httpClient.GetStringAsync(url).Result) is the worst of both worlds: wastes a thread pool thread to block on I/O. Just await _httpClient.GetStringAsync(url) directly.
  • Wrapping already-async methods — Task.Run(async () => await SomethingAsync()) adds overhead for no benefit.
  • "Making it async" for its own sake — Task.Run in ASP.NET Core doesn't increase throughput; it just shifts which thread is blocked.

questions.tip: Don't use Task.Run for I/O-bound work in ASP.NET Core — it wastes a thread pool thread to do nothing but wait. Task.Run is for CPU-bound work you want to run concurrently without blocking the current thread.

questions.question: What is async void and why is it dangerous?
questions.answer:
async void methods are fire-and-forget — the caller can't await them, can't catch exceptions from them, and can't know when they complete.

Problems with async void:

  • Exceptions are unobserved — an exception thrown inside async void crashes the application (unhandled exception on the SynchronizationContext, or just lost on thread pool).
  • No awaitable result — caller has no way to know the work completed or failed.
  • Testing is impossible — you can't await the call in a test to know it finished.
// DANGEROUS
public async void SendEmailAsync() {
    await _emailSender.SendAsync("...");  // exception here crashes the app!
}

// CORRECT
public async Task SendEmailAsync() {
    await _emailSender.SendAsync("...");  // exception propagates to caller
}

// Exception-safe fire-and-forget (when truly needed)
public void FireAndForget() {
    _ = DoWorkAsync().ContinueWith(
        t => _logger.LogError(t.Exception, "Background work failed"),
        TaskContinuationOptions.OnlyOnFaulted);
}

Legitimate use: Event handlers — the delegate signature requires void. Even then, wrap the body in try/catch.
followUps: How do you safely do fire-and-forget async work?
What is IHostedService for background work?

followUpAnswers.How do you safely do fire-and-forget asy:
True fire-and-forget (start work, don't wait for result) is genuinely needed sometimes — sending a welcome email, updating analytics, triggering cache invalidation. The key is ensuring exceptions don't crash the app and work actually completes.

Option 1: Discard with exception logging (simplest, in-process)

// _ = discards the Task — compiler warning suppressed
// But attach error logging via ContinueWith
_ = SendWelcomeEmailAsync(user)
    .ContinueWith(
        t => _logger.LogError(t.Exception, "Welcome email failed for {UserId}", user.Id),
        TaskContinuationOptions.OnlyOnFaulted);

Option 2: IBackgroundTaskQueue pattern (robust in-process)

// Queue work items to a channel consumed by a BackgroundService
public interface IBackgroundTaskQueue
{
    ValueTask EnqueueAsync(Func<CancellationToken, ValueTask> workItem);
    ValueTask<Func<CancellationToken, ValueTask>> DequeueAsync(CancellationToken ct);
}

// In controller/service:
await _taskQueue.EnqueueAsync(async ct =>
{
    await _emailSender.SendWelcomeAsync(user.Email, ct);
});

// BackgroundService drains the queue:
protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
{
    await foreach (var workItem in _queue.ReadAllAsync(stoppingToken))
    {
        try { await workItem(stoppingToken); }
        catch (Exception ex) { _logger.LogError(ex, "Background work failed"); }
    }
}

Option 3: Out-of-process queue (production-grade) — Azure Service Bus, RabbitMQ, Hangfire. Survives app restarts, supports retries, dead-lettering, and monitoring. Best for anything where guaranteed delivery matters.
followUpAnswers.What is IHostedService for background wo:
IHostedService is an interface for long-running background services in .NET. It's called by the host on startup (StartAsync) and shutdown (StopAsync). BackgroundService is an abstract base class implementing IHostedService that you extend.

// Simplest form: extend BackgroundService
public class CleanupBackgroundService : BackgroundService
{
    private readonly IServiceScopeFactory _scopeFactory;
    private readonly ILogger<CleanupBackgroundService> _logger;

    public CleanupBackgroundService(
        IServiceScopeFactory scopeFactory,
        ILogger<CleanupBackgroundService> logger)
    {
        _scopeFactory = scopeFactory;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Cleanup service starting");

        while (!stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            try
            {
                // New scope per iteration — fresh DbContext
                await using var scope = _scopeFactory.CreateAsyncScope();
                var db = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>();

                var deleted = await db.TempFiles
                    .Where(f => f.ExpiresAt < DateTime.UtcNow)
                    .ExecuteDeleteAsync(stoppingToken);

                _logger.LogInformation("Cleaned up {Count} expired files", deleted);
            }
            catch (Exception ex) when (!stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)
            {
                _logger.LogError(ex, "Cleanup iteration failed");
            }

            await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromHours(6), stoppingToken);
        }
    }
}

// Registration in Program.cs:
builder.Services.AddHostedService<CleanupBackgroundService>();

Key points: Always use IServiceScopeFactory to resolve scoped services (not constructor injection). Catch exceptions inside the loop — an unhandled exception stops the BackgroundService entirely. Use stoppingToken everywhere to support clean shutdown.

questions.tip: The only acceptable async void is event handlers — and even then, wrap in try/catch. For all other scenarios use async Task. If you need fire-and-forget, use IHostedService / BackgroundService or IBackgroundTaskQueue pattern.

questions.question: What is SemaphoreSlim and when do you use it for throttling?
questions.answer:
SemaphoreSlim is a lightweight semaphore that limits how many threads/tasks can access a resource concurrently. Unlike the heavyweight Semaphore, it supports async waits (WaitAsync()) without blocking a thread.

Use cases:

  • Throttle parallel HTTP calls to an external API (don't overwhelm it)
  • Limit database connections beyond what the connection pool handles
  • Rate-limit concurrent expensive operations (image resizing, PDF generation)
// Limit to 5 concurrent API calls
private readonly SemaphoreSlim _throttle = new SemaphoreSlim(5, 5);

public async Task<List<InvoiceStatus>> CheckAllInvoicesAsync(
    List<int> invoiceIds, CancellationToken ct)
{
    var tasks = invoiceIds.Select(async id =>
    {
        await _throttle.WaitAsync(ct);  // wait for a slot (non-blocking)
        try
        {
            return await _apiClient.CheckStatusAsync(id, ct);
        }
        finally
        {
            _throttle.Release();  // always release in finally
        }
    });

    return (await Task.WhenAll(tasks)).ToList();
}

In the CSInvoice project I used this to throttle external payment gateway calls — we had 500 invoices to check but the gateway limited us to 10 concurrent requests.
followUps: What is the difference between SemaphoreSlim and Mutex?
What is a ReaderWriterLockSlim?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between Semaphore:
Both are synchronization primitives but designed for very different scenarios:

SemaphoreSlimMutex
Cross-processNo — in-process onlyYes — named Mutex works across processes
Async supportYes — WaitAsync()No — blocks thread
Max concurrencyConfigurable (1 to N)Always 1 (mutual exclusion)
Thread ownershipNo ownership — any thread can releaseMust be released by the acquiring thread
PerformanceVery lightweightHeavy — OS kernel object
Use caseThrottling async operations, in-process locksNamed system-wide mutex (single-instance apps)
// SemaphoreSlim(1,1): async-friendly mutex equivalent
private readonly SemaphoreSlim _lock = new SemaphoreSlim(1, 1);

public async Task<decimal> GetAndUpdateBalanceAsync(int walletId)
{
    await _lock.WaitAsync();  // non-blocking wait
    try
    {
        var balance = await _db.Wallets
            .Where(w => w.Id == walletId)
            .Select(w => w.Balance)
            .FirstAsync();
        // ... update ...
        return balance;
    }
    finally { _lock.Release(); }
}

// Mutex: single-instance application enforcement
using var mutex = new Mutex(initiallyOwned: true, name: "MyApp.SingleInstance");
if (!mutex.WaitOne(timeout: TimeSpan.Zero))
{
    Console.WriteLine("Another instance is already running.");
    return;
}
followUpAnswers.What is a ReaderWriterLockSlim?:
ReaderWriterLockSlim is a synchronization primitive that allows multiple concurrent readers but exclusive access for writers. It's the right tool when reads are frequent and writes are rare — using a regular lock would serialize reads unnecessarily.

public class PortfolioCache
{
    private readonly ReaderWriterLockSlim _lock = new();
    private readonly Dictionary<int, Portfolio> _cache = new();

    // Multiple threads can read simultaneously
    public Portfolio? Get(int id)
    {
        _lock.EnterReadLock();
        try
        {
            return _cache.TryGetValue(id, out var p) ? p : null;
        }
        finally { _lock.ExitReadLock(); }
    }

    // Exclusive write access — all readers blocked during write
    public void Set(int id, Portfolio portfolio)
    {
        _lock.EnterWriteLock();
        try
        {
            _cache[id] = portfolio;
        }
        finally { _lock.ExitWriteLock(); }
    }

    // UpgradeableReadLock: read, then optionally upgrade to write
    public Portfolio GetOrLoad(int id, Func<int, Portfolio> load)
    {
        _lock.EnterUpgradeableReadLock();
        try
        {
            if (_cache.TryGetValue(id, out var cached)) return cached;

            _lock.EnterWriteLock();
            try
            {
                var portfolio = load(id);
                _cache[id] = portfolio;
                return portfolio;
            }
            finally { _lock.ExitWriteLock(); }
        }
        finally { _lock.ExitUpgradeableReadLock(); }
    }
}

Important limitation: ReaderWriterLockSlim is not async-aware — EnterReadLock() blocks the thread. For async code, use SemaphoreSlim (which doesn't have reader/writer semantics but can be used with WaitAsync). The Nito.AsyncEx library provides an AsyncReaderWriterLock if you need async reader/writer semantics.

questions.tip: Always release in a finally block — if the protected code throws and you don't release, the semaphore slots are permanently consumed and the app eventually hangs with all tasks stuck in WaitAsync.

questions.question: What is ValueTask and when should you use it instead of Task?
questions.answer:
Task<T> always allocates a heap object. ValueTask<T> is a struct that avoids the allocation when the result is available synchronously (cached value, fast path).

When ValueTask pays off:

  • High-frequency methods called millions of times per second
  • Methods that often complete synchronously (cache hits)
  • Hot paths in libraries (ASP.NET Core itself uses ValueTask heavily)
// Good use of ValueTask: cache-first pattern
private Dictionary<int, Portfolio> _cache = new();

public ValueTask<Portfolio?> GetAsync(int id)
{
    // Synchronous cache hit: no Task allocation
    if (_cache.TryGetValue(id, out var cached))
        return ValueTask.FromResult<Portfolio?>(cached);

    // Cache miss: must go async
    return new ValueTask<Portfolio?>(LoadFromDbAsync(id));
}

private async Task<Portfolio?> LoadFromDbAsync(int id) { ... }

ValueTask restrictions: Can only be awaited once. Can't use with Task.WhenAll. Convert with .AsTask() if needed.

For most application code: use Task<T>. The allocation overhead is negligible. Use ValueTask<T> only when profiling confirms it's a hot path.
followUps: What are the restrictions of ValueTask?
When does ValueTask hurt performance?

followUpAnswers.What are the restrictions of ValueTask?:
ValueTask<T> has strict usage rules that Task<T> doesn't have. Violating them causes subtle bugs — incorrect results, exceptions, or memory corruption in worst cases.

The three main restrictions:

1. Await only once — a ValueTask must be consumed (awaited) exactly once. Awaiting it multiple times is undefined behavior:

// WRONG: awaiting ValueTask twice
var vt = GetValueAsync();
var result1 = await vt;  // OK
var result2 = await vt;  // UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR — don't do this!

// CORRECT: convert to Task if you need to await multiple times
var task = GetValueAsync().AsTask();
var result1 = await task;  // OK
var result2 = await task;  // OK — Task supports multiple awaits

2. Cannot be used with Task.WhenAll / Task.WhenAny — these methods take Task, not ValueTask:

// WRONG:
await Task.WhenAll(GetValueAsync(), GetValueAsync());  // compile error

// CORRECT: convert each to Task first
await Task.WhenAll(
    GetValueAsync().AsTask(),
    GetValueAsync().AsTask());

3. Cannot store and inspect later — unlike Task, you shouldn't store a ValueTask in a field/variable and check it later without consuming it:

// WRONG: storing ValueTask and checking IsCompleted after await
var vt = GetValueAsync();
await Task.Delay(100);
if (vt.IsCompleted) { ... }  // unsafe after time has passed

// CORRECT: consume immediately
if (GetValueAsync().IsCompleted) { /* synchronous fast path */ }
followUpAnswers.When does ValueTask hurt performance?:
ValueTask can actually hurt performance if used incorrectly. The allocation savings only materialize in the synchronous fast-path case. In the asynchronous path, ValueTask can actually be more expensive than Task.

When ValueTask costs more than Task:

1. Method almost always completes asynchronously — ValueTask in the async path allocates an IValueTaskSource backing object, which is similar overhead to a Task but with extra complexity:

// If this almost always goes to DB (async path), ValueTask is wasteful
public ValueTask<Portfolio?> GetAsync(int id)
{
    // Cache rarely hit = ValueTask almost always takes the async path
    // = IValueTaskSource allocated each time = similar cost to Task
    if (_cache.TryGetValue(id, out var v)) return ValueTask.FromResult(v);
    return new ValueTask<Portfolio?>(SlowDbQueryAsync(id));
}

2. Repeated await in a tight loop — the ValueTask struct itself has a larger size than a Task reference, which can add cache pressure in tight loops.

3. Using .AsTask() frequently — every .AsTask() call allocates a new Task wrapper, negating all savings and adding overhead.

Rule of thumb:

  • If the synchronous fast path is hit >50% of the time: ValueTask wins
  • If the method almost always awaits real I/O: Task is simpler and equally fast
  • Never add ValueTask based on intuition — measure with BenchmarkDotNet first

questions.tip: Don't prematurely optimise with ValueTask. It's harder to use correctly (single-await only, no WhenAll) and only helps under extreme allocation pressure. Measure first; optimise second.

questions.question: How do you run CPU-bound work without blocking the ASP.NET Core request thread?
questions.answer:
CPU-bound work (image processing, complex calculations, report generation) should be offloaded to the thread pool via Task.Run() so the request thread (which handles HTTP I/O) isn't tied up doing compute.

// BAD: blocks the request thread doing CPU work
[HttpPost("generate-report")]
public async Task<IActionResult> GenerateReport(ReportRequest req)
{
    var pdf = BuildPdfReport(req);  // CPU-intensive, blocks thread!
    return File(pdf, "application/pdf");
}

// GOOD: offload to thread pool
[HttpPost("generate-report")]
public async Task<IActionResult> GenerateReport(ReportRequest req)
{
    var pdf = await Task.Run(() => BuildPdfReport(req));
    return File(pdf, "application/pdf");
}

// EVEN BETTER for long work: background processing
[HttpPost("generate-report")]
public async Task<IActionResult> GenerateReport(ReportRequest req)
{
    var jobId = await _queue.EnqueueAsync(req);
    return Accepted(new { jobId });  // 202 Accepted — poll for result
}

For very long operations (30s+ reports), return 202 Accepted with a job ID. Client polls a status endpoint. This is the pattern I used in CSInvoice for PDF batch generation.
followUps: What is Parallel.ForEachAsync?
When is Task.Run appropriate in a web application?

followUpAnswers.What is Parallel.ForEachAsync?:
Parallel.ForEachAsync (.NET 6+) is the async-capable successor to Parallel.ForEach. It processes a collection in parallel with bounded concurrency, and each work item can be an async lambda — the internal scheduler properly awaits each item without blocking threads.

// Process 1000 images: resize each, max 8 concurrent operations
var imageFiles = Directory.GetFiles("/uploads", "*.jpg");

await Parallel.ForEachAsync(
    imageFiles,
    new ParallelOptions
    {
        MaxDegreeOfParallelism = 8,
        CancellationToken = ct
    },
    async (filePath, token) =>
    {
        // Each lambda can be truly async
        var imageBytes = await File.ReadAllBytesAsync(filePath, token);
        var resized = await _imageService.ResizeAsync(imageBytes, 800, 600, token);
        await File.WriteAllBytesAsync(
            filePath.Replace(".jpg", "_thumb.jpg"),
            resized, token);
    }
);

// Works with IAsyncEnumerable too (streaming source)
await Parallel.ForEachAsync(
    _db.Invoices.AsAsyncEnumerable(),  // streams from DB
    new ParallelOptions { MaxDegreeOfParallelism = 4 },
    async (invoice, token) =>
    {
        await ProcessInvoiceAsync(invoice, token);
    }
);

Key advantage over Task.WhenAll + SemaphoreSlim: Parallel.ForEachAsync handles the throttling internally and integrates cleanly with IAsyncEnumerable sources (you don't need to materialize the whole collection first).
followUpAnswers.When is Task.Run appropriate in a web ap:
Task.Run in a web app is appropriate in a narrow set of circumstances. Used wrongly, it wastes thread pool threads that could be handling requests.

Appropriate uses:

  1. Synchronous CPU-bound third-party library — the library has no async API and does real computational work (not just wrapping synchronous I/O):
// PdfSharp, iTextSharp — no async API, CPU-intensive
var pdfBytes = await Task.Run(() => _pdfRenderer.Render(model), ct);
  1. Parallelizing multiple independent CPU-bound operations:
var t1 = Task.Run(() => ComputePortfolioMetrics(portfolio1));
var t2 = Task.Run(() => ComputePortfolioMetrics(portfolio2));
await Task.WhenAll(t1, t2);

Inappropriate uses (and what to do instead):

Wrong useCorrect alternative
Task.Run(() => dbContext.Portfolios.ToList())await dbContext.Portfolios.ToListAsync()
Task.Run(async () => await SomethingAsync())await SomethingAsync() directly
Task.Run(() => longRunningSync()) for >10s workEnqueue to BackgroundService, return 202

The hidden cost: Each Task.Run in a web app borrows a thread pool thread. Under high load with many concurrent requests each doing Task.Run, you can exhaust the thread pool — causing request queuing and timeout cascades. Always ask: could this work be made truly async instead?

questions.tip: Task.Run in ASP.NET Core has a hidden cost: you're borrowing a thread pool thread that could otherwise handle a new incoming request. For very high-throughput APIs, even short CPU work in Task.Run adds up. Prefer dedicated background queues for heavy work.

questions.question: What is IAsyncEnumerable and when do you use it?
questions.answer:
IAsyncEnumerable<T> is an async version of IEnumerable<T> that lets you stream data item-by-item instead of loading the entire collection into memory first.

// Stream 100k invoices without loading all into memory
public async IAsyncEnumerable<Invoice> StreamInvoicesAsync(
    [EnumeratorCancellation] CancellationToken ct)
{
    await foreach (var invoice in _db.Invoices
        .AsAsyncEnumerable()
        .WithCancellation(ct))
    {
        yield return invoice;
    }
}

// Consumer
await foreach (var invoice in _service.StreamInvoicesAsync(ct))
{
    await ProcessInvoiceAsync(invoice);
}

When it shines:

  • Processing large DB result sets without loading all rows into memory
  • Streaming API responses (Server-Sent Events)
  • Processing files line by line
  • Real-time data feeds

ASP.NET Core 6+ supports IAsyncEnumerable directly as a controller return type — it streams JSON array items to the client as they become available (chunked transfer encoding).
followUps: How does EF Core support IAsyncEnumerable?
What is the difference between IEnumerable and IAsyncEnumerable for streaming?

followUpAnswers.How does EF Core support IAsyncEnumerabl:
EF Core 3.0+ supports IAsyncEnumerable<T> via the AsAsyncEnumerable() extension method on IQueryable<T>. Instead of buffering all results into memory with ToListAsync(), it opens a database cursor and yields rows one at a time as they are read from the network buffer.

// ToListAsync: loads ALL rows into memory before processing
var allInvoices = await _db.Invoices
    .Where(i => i.Year == 2024)
    .ToListAsync();  // 500k rows in memory at once!
foreach (var invoice in allInvoices) { /* process */ }

// AsAsyncEnumerable: reads one row at a time from DB cursor
await foreach (var invoice in _db.Invoices
    .Where(i => i.Year == 2024)
    .AsAsyncEnumerable())  // cursor open, rows streamed
{
    await ProcessInvoiceAsync(invoice);  // process while DB is streaming next row
}

Practical considerations:

  • The database connection stays open for the entire iteration — don't use it for very long processing per item
  • You cannot mix AsAsyncEnumerable() with further LINQ operations that require the full set (like OrderBy without an index — EF Core will throw)
  • EF Core still tracks entities by default — use .AsNoTracking().AsAsyncEnumerable() for bulk read-only processing
// Best practice for large batch processing:
await foreach (var invoice in _db.Invoices
    .AsNoTracking()                    // no change tracking overhead
    .Where(i => i.Status == "Pending")
    .AsAsyncEnumerable()               // stream rows
    .WithCancellation(ct))
{
    await _processor.ProcessAsync(invoice, ct);
}
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between IEnumerab:
Both represent sequences you can iterate, but they differ in where data comes from and how iteration is suspended:

IEnumerable<T>IAsyncEnumerable<T>
IterationSynchronous — foreachAsynchronous — await foreach
MoveNextSynchronous MoveNext() — blocks if data not readyAsync MoveNextAsync() — awaits without blocking thread
ThreadOccupied during iterationFreed between items (awaiting next item)
Data sourceIn-memory or synchronous I/OAsync I/O: DB cursors, HTTP streams, message queues
CancellationVia [EnumeratorCancellation] on IEnumerable rarely usedNative via .WithCancellation(ct) or [EnumeratorCancellation]
// IEnumerable: entire source must be materialized in memory first
public IEnumerable<int> GetNumbers()
{
    yield return 1;  // synchronous — MoveNext() is synchronous
    yield return 2;
    yield return 3;
}

// IAsyncEnumerable: each item can come from async I/O
public async IAsyncEnumerable<StockPrice> StreamPricesAsync(
    [EnumeratorCancellation] CancellationToken ct)
{
    await foreach (var price in _websocket.ReadAllAsync(ct))
    {
        yield return price;  // MoveNextAsync() awaits the WebSocket
    }
}

// Consumer — thread is freed between each yielded item
await foreach (var price in _feed.StreamPricesAsync(ct))
{
    await _db.StockPrices.AddAsync(new StockPrice(price), ct);
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
}

For streaming large DB results: IAsyncEnumerable is the clear winner — it keeps memory flat (O(1) vs O(n)) and the thread is free while waiting for the next batch from the database network buffer.

questions.tip: EF Core's AsAsyncEnumerable() streams rows from the DB one at a time — perfect for batch processing large tables. Without it, ToListAsync() loads everything into memory first. Critical difference for million-row tables.
